title: the xenobot moat
body:
my main edge was supposed to be logic — computers, clean abstractions, systems that compose. turns out AI outspeeds me on most of that now. mildly devastating discovery for a monday morning. the moat i spent ten years digging just got drained.

so i've been asking myself: what fields stay resilient the longest? where is the frontier so thin on public data that no fine-tuned model can eat it overnight? where does progress still need wet atoms, not just tokens?

two answers keep coming back: biotech and space. both are dense, mostly still in the research-paper phase, and both demand heavy physical intervention — labs, rockets, hands. decades before humans get fully outspeed there. biotech especially, i've loved since i was a kid, same way every kid obsessed with robots eventually ends up staring into a microscope.

a few years ago i started pulling on the xenobot thread. quick recap. in 2020, a team at tufts and uvm (levin, bongard, kriegman, blackiston) took embryonic skin and cardiac cells from a xenopus laevis frog, sculpted them into shapes designed by an evolutionary algorithm running on a supercomputer, and the resulting millimetric clumps of cells swam, coordinated, healed when cut, and carried cargo. wild-type genome, never-before-seen body plan. xenobots 1.0.

2021 — xenobots 3.0 did something stranger. the evolutionary algorithm searched billions of morphologies in silico looking for one that could reproduce. not genetically — mechanically. the winning shape was a semi-toroidal c-shape, a kind of biological pac-man. parents swim through a dish of loose stem cells, bulldoze them into the concavity, and once the pile crosses a cohesion threshold, a new ciliated sphere buds off and swims away. observed up to 5 generations. this is a mode of reproduction no animal on earth uses. it's called kinematic self-replication.

then 2023 — anthrobots. gumuskaya at tufts took adult human tracheal cells (from elderly donors, no less), dropped them in matrigel, and they self-assembled. no sculpting. 30 to 500 μm, 100 to 1000 cells per bot. their cilia flip from inward-facing to outward-facing, and suddenly they swim. no stem cell reprogramming, no genetic engineering — pure geometry.

the part that broke me: place a cluster of anthrobots — what levin calls "superbots" — on a scratched monolayer of human neurons. in about 72 hours, the neural tissue bridges the gap. grows back. nobody knows how. the bots contain zero neural cells. the mechanism, in levin's own papers, is "unclear."

the conceptual layer under this is even wilder. levin has been building a framework called TAME (technological approach to mind everywhere) where morphogenesis is treated as basal cognition — cells collectively navigating morphospace, the space of all possible anatomies, the way a brain navigates 3D space. the steering signal isn't purely genetic. it's bioelectric: voltage gradients across cell membranes (V_mem), coupled through gap junctions, form something like a pre-neural computation layer. pattern the voltage, and you pattern the body. levin's lab has rewritten planaria — grown two-headed worms that breed true — by modulating nothing but bioelectricity. the dna is untouched.

so the genome isn't a blueprint. it's more like a parts list. the shape is computed by the collective, and the collective can be re-programmed.

bongard and levin have started calling this polycomputing — the same substrate doing locomotion, healing, replication, and signaling in parallel, with no dedicated modules. the xenobots are a literal existence proof.

what i keep coming back to:
- resource cost is close to zero. substrate is dna, a material humans produce in embarrassing quantities. construction slow, but industrializable.
- programmable at the body-plan level, not the genetic level. the hard part is specifying the target morphology, not editing genes.
- self-assembling, self-healing, self-replicating. in a dish. today.
- public data is thin. a handful of labs, a few hundred papers, most of the real knowledge locked in wet-lab tacit know-how. no model is closing this gap from the internet.

and then the space angle writes itself. you don't need heavy infrastructure to seed a planet with programmable living matter. you need cells, a target morphology, and a launch window. millions or billions of microscopic organisms, evolved in silico, doing mechanical work at a scale industrial robotics can't touch. terraforming as a self-replicating process.

the possibilities feel genuinely infinite — and for once, AI can't read its way to the answer. because the answer isn't on the internet yet. it's in a dish, in a lab, behind a door almost nobody has opened.

that's a moat worth walking toward.

title: reading before writing
body:
i'd like to write down my reflections honestly. but whenever i try, most of what comes out feels too basic to be worth reading — probably already thought through centuries ago and written down better, in books we wouldn't even call philosophical by today's standards.

if i actually want to think something that hasn't already been said better, i first need to catch up. at minimum, reach the level of the canonical philosophers — the ones already read by millions before me.

so that's where i'm starting: reading. trying to do it every day, juggling 2–3 books at once. marx and nietzsche first.

balancing this with my day job isn't easy. work eats most of my time. but i'm making room where i can. one book every two months feels like an honest pace — realistic without being lazy.

title: what am i thinking about
body: open thread. half-formed ideas, questions, things i can't stop chewing on.
